content.full_text:
South Coast
AIR QUALTTY MANAGEMENT DISTR|CT
21865 E. Copley Drive, Diamond Bar, CA 91 765-4182 (714) 396-2000
^-l
OFFICE OF DISTRICT COUNSEL
P. O. Box 4940
Diamond Bar, CA 9U654940
Writer's Direct Dial:
(7r4) 396-2303
Fax: (714) 396-2961
March 31, 1992
Mr. Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
California Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807
Re: Request for Formal Written Advice on Behalf of the South Coast Air Quality
Management District (SCAQMD) and the Mobile Source Air Pollution
Reduction Review Committee (MSRC) on whether the MSRC is Subject to
the Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
On March 19, 1992, John Wallace of your office and Principal Deputy District
Counsel Barbara Baird and Senior Deputy District Counsel Denice Brue of my
office discussed whether or not the Mobile Source Air Pollution Reduction Review
Committee is governed by the disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act.
Ms. Baird and Ms. Brue analyzed this issue in light of the Rotman opinion (10 FPPC
Opinions 1). The result of their analysis indicated that the issue was not clear.
Therefore, the advice of your office is solicited.
QUESTIONS PRESENTED
1. 
Are persons serving on the Mobile Source Air Pollution Reduction Review
Committee -(MSRC) (Health and Saf. Code Sec. 44244) "members" within the
meaninq of 2 Cal. Code Reg. Sec. 18700 merely by virtue of the fact that the District
Governlng Board may rejeci their recommenditi6ns onty by a3f 4 vote, even though
the MSRC is not a committee of the District?
2. 
If the answer to question 1 is yes, should the District amend its conflict of
interest code to include MSRC members, or should the MSRC itself adopt a conflict
of interest code?


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March 31, 1992
Page2
3. 
Are staff members serving on the Technical. Advisory Committee (TAC) to
the MSRC (Health and Saf. Code Sec. 44244(c))t required to file statements of
economic interest with the South Coast Air Quality Management District by virtue
of their service on the TAC?
BACKGROUND
The South Coast Air Quality Management District (District) is a regional
governmental agenry encompassing the counties of [,os Angeles, Orange and
Riverside and the non-desert portions of San Bernardino county. The District was
established by state law (Health and Saf. Code Secs.4M00 et seq.) and is the agency
responsible for comprehensive air pollution control plans and programs at the local
level within the South Coast Air Basin. (Sec.4M00(h).) Except as more specific
statutes provide additional or different authority, it is subject to the general
provisions relating to air pollution control districts contained in Division 26 of the
Health and Safety Code.
Among the District's responsibilities is the obligation to adopt and implement an air
quality management plan designed to achieve state standards for four specific
tollutants by the earliest practicable date. as required by the California Clean Air
Act. (Sec. 40910.) In 1988, the lrgislature adopted the California Clean Air Act
(Chapter 10 of Part 3, Division 26) which requires nonattainment districts such as
the SCAQMD to "focus particular attention on reducing the emissions from
transportation and areawide sources" (Sec.40910) in adopting and implementing
planf to achieve the state ambient air quality standards. (Sec. 40910.) Specific
requirements for adopting transportation control measures are set out for the
districts in Sections 40918 - 40920, depending on whether the district's level of
pollution is characterized as "moderate," "serious," or "severe." The new emphasis
bn transportation control measures and reducing pollution for vehicular sources
posed a funding problem for districts, many of which traditionally received a large
portion of their funds from stationary source permit fees. The California
l-"egislature in 1990 adopted AB 2'766 to provide a source of funding for these newly-
required programs. Within the South Coast district, 30Vo of the funds are to be
expended by the district, 40Vo by cities and counties which bear responsibility for
implementation of many transportation control measures under the region's air
quality management plan, and 30Vo are to be expended pursuant to a work program
adopted by the Mobile Source Air Pollution Reduction Review Committee (MSRC)
and approved by the District's Coverning Board. (Sec.44243.)
Division 26 of the Health and Safety Code (Air Resources) was amended in 1990 to
add Chapter 7 of Part 5 ("District Fees to Implement the California Clean Air Act"),
Sections 44220 - 44247. Chapter 7 was added by Stats. 1990, c. 1705 (A82766) and
is frequently referred to as "AB 27 66." The law authorizes a nonattainment air
pollution control district to impose a fee of up to two dollars (four dollars beginning
April 1, 1992) on motor vehicles registered within the district. (Health and Saf.
Code Secs. 44223,44225.) The fees are collected by the Department of Motor
Vehicles upon renewal of the registration of a motor vehicle registered in the
district. (Sec. 44227.) (A complete copy of Chapter 7 is enclosed for your
reference.) Fee revenues generated within the South Coast Air Quality
1 Unless otherwise specified all section references are to the Health and Safety Code.


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March 31, 1992
Page 3
Management District are to be subvened to the District for expenditure in three
separate categories. (Sec.44243.) Thirty cents of every dollar is to be used by the
South Coast Air Quality Management District ("District") for programs to reduce air
pollution from motor vehicles and to carry out related planning, monitoring,
enforcement and technical studies authorized by or necessary to implement the
California Clean Air Act or the District's air quality management plan. (Sec.
aa2a3@).) Forty cents of every dollar is distributed by the District to the cities and
counties within the district based upon their prorated share of population to be used
to implement programs to reduce air pollution from motor vehicles which are
authorized by or necessary to implement the California Clean Air Act or the
District's air quality management plan. (Sec. 44243(b).) The use oi these funds is
left to the discretion of the respective cities and counties, except that the District is
to audit the use of the funds by the cities and counties to assure the funds are used
for a purpose authorized by statute. (Sec.44244.1.) The third share of the funds is
the portion which prompts this inquiry. Thirty cents of every dollar is to be
deposited by the District in an account to be used, pursuant to Section 44244, to
implement or monitor programs to reduce air pollution from motor vehicles which
are authorized by, or necessary to implement, the California Clean Air Act or the
District's air quality management plan. (Sec. aa2a3@).)
Section 44244, which governs the distribution of funds from this third portion of AB
2766 revenues, provides as follows:
'S ,14244. Regional mobile sourcc air pollution roduction review committee; mcmbors; use of
funds for work program; technical advisory committee
(u)
There is hereby created a regional Mobile Source Air Pollution Reduction
Review Committee. The committee shall be comprised of one representative from each of the
following agencies:
(1) 
The south coast district.
The Southern California Association of Governments.
The San Bernardino Associated Govcrnments.
The Los Angeles County Transportation Commission.
The Orange County Transportation Commission.
The Riverside County Transportation Commission.
The state board. [.e., Air Rcsources Board.]
A regional ridesharing agency selected by the other members of the committee.
Fees allocated pursuant to subdivision (c) of Section 44243 shall be used to fund
projects pursuant to a work program developed and adopted by the committee and approved
by the south coast district board in the following manner:
(1)
The work program shall be adopted by an afhrmative vote of a majority of the
(z)
(3)
(4)
(s)
(6)
(7)
(8)
(b)
committec members.


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March 31, 1992
Page 4
(2) 
Upon adoption of the work program, the work program shall be submitted to the
south coast district board which, within (r0 days, shall approve or disapprovc thc work
program. If the district board fails to approve of disapprove the work program within 60 days
of receiving it, the work program shall be deemed approved. The district board may
disapprove the work program only upon a three-fourths vote of the full district board. If the
district board disapproves the work program, it shall be returned to the mmmittee which shall
amend, readopt, and resubmit the work program to the district board for approval or
disapproval.
(c)
Thc committee shall establish a technical advisory committee to assist in the
2 The Corn^ittee is commonly referred to by an abbreviated acronym, as the "MSRC."
r The work program was developed with the assistance of the MSRC's technical advisory committee,
which is also required by the governing statute, Section 442'14(c), and District staff.
development of the work program. The technical advisory committee shall includc, but not be
limited to, representatives of agencies which make up the committee, a representative of the
cities from each county within the district, and a representative of the boards of supcrvisors o[
each county within the district. The technical advisory committee may also include
representatives o[ other public agencies and other intcrested parties as the committee may
determine to bc appropriate.
(d) 
The south coast district shall not bc cligible for funds allocated pursuant to this
section."
The representatives of each agency serving on the Committee were each appointed
by their respective appointing authority. The Mobile Source Air Pollution
Reduction Review Committee' is not a committee of the District, nor does the
District have any say in determining what agencies will be represented on the
committee or who will be the representative from each agency appointed to the
committee (except its own representative).
The MSRC was constituted in 1991 and that year developed a work program for the
expenditure of FY 1991-1992 revenues attributed to the Sec.tion aa2a3@) share of
the dollars received by the District pursuant to AB 27661 The work program
provides for the distribution of approximately 6.8 million dollars among 41 different
projects. Following adoption of the work program by the MSRC, the District Board
approved the work program at its December 6, 1991 meeting. The work program
(attached as Exhibit 1) was adopted following issuance by the District of a request
for proposals (attached as Exhibit 2) calling for interested persons and agencies to
submit proposals for using portions of the MSRC funding to reduce pollution from
motor vehicles. Forty-one projects (out of 118 submitted) were selected; many of
them did not receive all the funding they requested. Now that the work program has
been approved by the District Board the funds are being distributed through
contracls entered into belween the District and the sponsor of each project selected.
MSRC members are not compensated by the District for their involvement on the
committee.
The technical advisory committee ('TAC') to the MSRC (Sec. a4244(c)) consists of
staff members of public agencies as set out in Section 44244(c) and the regional
ridesharing agency which is represented on MSRC. As a practical matter, some of
the TAC members are more active than others in the work necessary to assist the


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March31,, 1992
Page 5
MSRC in selecting projects for funding. The District does not pay TAC members
for their work on the committee. We have not discussed this issue with all the TAC
members, but we know that at least some of them are not designated in their own
agency's conflict of interest code. We are concerned that requiring these staff
peopl6 to file disclosure statements could seriously inhibit the MSRC's ability to find
iledicated, hardworking and professional staff members to serve on the TAC. We
believe staff members serving on the TAC would not come within the scope of any
part of Section 18700 of the regulation. since any work they do is subject to
intervening substantive review by the MSRC prior to being forwarded to the final
decisionmaker, the District Board.
In reviewing the relevant FPPC regulations, we believe it is clear that persons
serving on the MSRC are not officers, employees, or consultants of the District. (2
Cal. eode Reg. 18700(a).) Moreover, the MSRC itself is not a public entity
constituting a aeparate legal entity. However, we request your advice concerning
whether thbse who serve on the MSRC could be considered "members" within the
meaning of Section 18700 of the regulations. Even though none of the three factors
listed in Section 18700(a)(1) appears directly applicable, the Rotman opinion
appears to place great importance on the fact that the ultimate decisionmaker could
reject the committee's recommendation only with a supermajority vote. On the
other hand, the committee involved in Rotman (project area committee of a
redevelopment agency) is selected pursuant to a procedure adopted by the agency
and is subject to the approval of the agency (Health and Saf. Code Sec. 33385(b)
and 33385(d)), whereas the MSRC is neither selected by the District nor subject to
approval by the District.
SUMMARY OF KEY FACTS
1. 
The MSRC was created by Section 44244 of the Health and Safety Code.
Health and Safety Code Section 44244(a) specifies the agencies which shall provide
representation to comprise the MSRC. The District has no authority over either the
agencies which are to provide representatives or the identity of the representatives
selected.
2. 
Health and Safety Code Section 44244(b) provides that the committee shall
adopt work programs for expenditure of vehicle registration fees. The programs are
subject to approval by the District Board.
3. 
Pursuant to Section 44244(b)(2), the District Board can disapprove the work
program only by 314 vote of the full District Board.
4. 
Each agency enumerated in Section 44244(a) appoints a representative tc)
the MSRC. fie only entity which does not represent a pubtic entity is the regional
ridesharing agency which is selected by the members of the committee. The
ridesharing agency which has been selected is a private non-profit agency.
5. 
The work program was developed by a Request for Proposal process (see
attachment 1). Excepl for the District (Sec. aazaa(\), agencies whose members are
a part of the MSRC may also submit a proposal for funding which must be approved
by the MSRC.


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March 31, 1992
Page 6
6. 
Currently, the MSRC members are not designated in the District's conflict of
interest code.
7. 
Many of the MSRC members are subject to disclosure requirements as the
result of membership on a local agency governing board or as a result of
membership on one of the entities enumerated in Section 44244(a) of the Health
and Safety Code. The jurisdiction of the District encompasses four counties - [-os
Angeles, Orange, Riverside and the non-desert portions of San Bernardino county.
ExCept for the Southern California Association of Governments and the Air
Resources Board, the entities enumerated in Section 44244(a) have more limited
geographical jurisdictions. Staff persons who serve on the TAC are less likely to be
ilesignated in their agency's conflict of interest code.
8. 
The MSRC votes on the work program which is comprised of individual
projects to be funded pursuant to Health and Safety Code Section 44244(b). The
MSRC cannot unilaterally commit the District to a particular course of action. The
contracts which result from the RFP process are between the District and the
recipient of the funds (contractor) approved pursuant to the proposal submitted.
CONCLUSION
Therefore, it is requested that formal written advice be given on whether the MSRC
or the TAC is subject to the disclosure requirements of the Political Reform Act.
In discussing this with John Wallace, he indicated, and this is to confirm, that in the
event that the MSRC is determined to be subject to the disclosure requirements,
that the required statement would be treated as assuming office statement which
would not subject the MSRC committee to any penalties for late filing after April 1,
1992. Nevertheless, we would request an expedited opinion in this matter.
Thank you for y our prompt attention tt) this matter. Please contact the undersigned
at (714) 396-2303 if you need any further information.
District Counsel
BBB:DEB:vmr
Attachments
msrcfppc
Sincerely,

1. 
Are persons serving on the Mobile Source Air Pollution Reduction Review
Committee -(MSRC) (Health and Saf. Code Sec. 44244) "members" within the
meaninq of 2 Cal. Code Reg. Sec. 18700 merely by virtue of the fact that the District
Governlng Board may rejeci their recommenditi6ns onty by a3f 4 vote, even though
the MSRC is not a committee of the District?
2. 
If the answer to question 1 is yes, should the District amend its conflict of
interest code to include MSRC members, or should the MSRC itself adopt a conflict
of interest code?


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March 31, 1992
Page2
3. 
Are staff members serving on the Technical. Advisory Committee (TAC) to
the MSRC (Health and Saf. Code Sec. 44244(c))t required to file statements of
economic interest with the South Coast Air Quality Management District by virtue
of their service on the TAC?
sections.conclusion:
Therefore, it is requested that formal written advice be given on whether the MSRC
or the TAC is subject to the disclosure requirements of the Political Reform Act.
In discussing this with John Wallace, he indicated, and this is to confirm, that in the
event that the MSRC is determined to be subject to the disclosure requirements,
that the required statement would be treated as assuming office statement which
would not subject the MSRC committee to any penalties for late filing after April 1,
1992. Nevertheless, we would request an expedited opinion in this matter.
Thank you for y our prompt attention tt) this matter. Please contact the undersigned
at (714) 396-2303 if you need any further information.
District Counsel

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. 
Are persons serving on the Mobile Source Air Pollution Reduction Review
Committee -(MSRC) (Health and Saf. Code Sec. 44244) "members" within the
meaninq of 2 Cal. Code Reg. Sec. 18700 merely by virtue of the fact that the District
Governlng Board may rejeci their recommenditi6ns onty by a3f 4 vote, even though
the MSRC is not a committee of the District?
2. 
If the answer to question 1 is yes, should the District amend its conflict of
interest code to include MSRC members, or should the MSRC itself adopt a conflict
of interest code?


Mr. Scott Hallabrin
March 31, 1992
Page2
3. 
Are staff members serving on the Technical. Advisory Committee (TAC) to
the MSRC (Health and Saf. Code Sec. 44244(c))t required to file statements of
economic interest with the South Coast Air Quality Management District by virtue
of their service on the TAC?

CONCLUSION: Therefore, it is requested that formal written advice be given on whether the MSRC
or the TAC is subject to the disclosure requirements of the Political Reform Act.
In discussing this with John Wallace, he indicated, and this is to confirm, that in the
event that the MSRC is determined to be subject to the disclosure requirements,
that the required statement would be treated as assuming office statement which
would not subject the MSRC committee to any penalties for late filing after April 1,
1992. Nevertheless, we would request an expedited opinion in this matter.
Thank you for y our prompt attention tt) this matter. Please contact the undersigned
at (714) 396-2303 if you need any further information.
District Counsel

Box 4940 Diamond Bar, CA 9U654940 Writer's Direct Dial: (7r4) 396-2303 Fax: (714) 396-2961 March 31, 1992 Mr. Scott Hallabrin General Counsel California Fair Political Practices Commission 428 J Street, Suite 800 Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 Re: Request for Formal Written Advice on Behalf of the South Coast Air Quality Management District (SCAQMD) and the Mobile Source Air Pollution Reduction Review Committee (MSRC) on whether the MSRC is Subject to the Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform Act Dear Mr. Hallabrin: On March 19, 1992, John Wallace of your office and Principal Deputy District Counsel Barbara Baird and Senior Deputy District Counsel Denice Brue of my office discussed whether or not the Mobile Source Air Pollution Reduction Review Committee is governed by the disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act. Ms. Baird and Ms. Brue analyzed this issue in light of the Rotman opinion (10 FPPC Opinions 1). The result of their analysis indicated that the issue was not clear. Therefore, the advice of your office is solicited. QUESTIONS PRESENTED 1. Are persons serving on the Mobile Source Air Pollution Reduction Review Committee -(MSRC) (Health and Saf. Code Sec. 44244) "members" within the meaninq of 2 Cal. Code Reg. Sec. 18700 merely by virtue of the fact that the District Governlng Board may rejeci their recommenditi6ns onty by a3f 4 vote, even though the MSRC is not a committee of the District? 2. If the answer to question 1 is yes, should the District amend its conflict of interest code to include MSRC members, or should the MSRC itself adopt a conflict of interest code? Mr. Scott Hallabrin March 31, 1992 Page2 3. Are staff members serving on the Technical. Advisory Committee (TAC) to the MSRC (Health and Saf. Code Sec. 44244(c))t required to file statements of economic interest with the South Coast Air Quality Management District by virtue of their service on the TAC? BACKGROUND The South Coast Air Quality Management District (District) is a regional governmental agenry encompassing the counties of [,os Angeles, Orange and Riverside and the non-desert portions of San Bernardino county. The District was established by state law (Health and Saf. Code Secs.4M00 et seq.) and is the agency responsible for comprehensive air pollution control plans and programs at the local level within the South Coast Air Basin. (Sec.4M00(h).) Except as more specific statutes provide additional or different authority, it is subject to the general provisions relating to air pollution control districts contained in Division 26 of the Health and Safety Code. Among the District's responsibilities is the obligation to adopt and implement an air quality management plan designed to achieve state standards for four specific tollutants by the earliest practicable date. as required by the California Clean Air Act. (Sec. 40910.) In 1988, the lrgislature adopted the California Clean Air Act (Chapter 10 of Part 3, Division 26) which requires nonattainment
